serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of vegetarian crispy spring rolls consists of approximately 3 pieces, weighing around 120 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Spring rolls, vegetable, fried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for fried vegetable spring rolls. The dish described (minced mushroom, tofu, vegetables in rice paper, deep-fried) is compositionally consistent with USDA fried vegetable spring roll entries. Values represent a reasonable estimate for this Vietnamese-style vegetarian variant served in Singapore. No exact HPB Singapore Foodo entry was found for this specific dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese-style vegetarian crispy spring rolls. GI estimated at 50 based on analogous fried spring rolls / spring rolls with vegetable filling reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Rice paper wrapper contributes moderate starch; deep-frying and fibre from vegetables moderate the glycaemic response. GL per serving calculated as (50 × 31.7 g carbohydrate per 120 g serving) / 100 = 15.8.

hypertension.guidance: Each 120 g serving provides approximately 456 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid additional dipping sauces high in sodium such as soy sauce or fish sauce.

type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 50 and a glycaemic load of about 15.8 per serving, vegetarian crispy spring rolls have a moderate glycaemic impact; people with Type 2 Diabetes should limit to one serving and pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat and sodium from deep-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Deep-frying contributes approximately 10.8 g fat per 100 g including 2.1 g saturated fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally and in small portions, preferring baked or air-fried alternatives where available.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetarian Crispy Spring Rolls (Vietnamese: Chả Giò Chay) are deep-fried rolls made by wrapping a savoury filling of minced mushrooms, tofu, shredded cabbage, carrot, and glass noodles in thin rice paper, then frying until golden and crisp. The exterior is shatteringly crunchy while the interior is soft, umami-rich, and lightly seasoned. This dish is a vegetarian and vegan adaptation of the classic Vietnamese Chả Giò (pork spring rolls), widely served in Vietnamese restaurants across Singapore. The rice paper wrapper becomes translucent and blistered when fried, giving a distinctive texture. Nutritionally, each 120 g serving provides approximately 262 kcal, 6.2 g protein, 13.0 g fat, and 31.7 g carbohydrate. The dish is moderate in sodium (~456 mg per serving) and contains dietary fibre (~2.8 g per serving) from the vegetable filling. The glycaemic index is estimated at 50 (low GI), making it a moderate glycaemic load food. It is suitable for vegetarians and vegans, and is generally halal-friendly. Clinically, the sodium content warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension, and the carbohydrate load should be monitored by those with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is best consumed in moderation as part of a balanced meal.
common_user_queries: What are vegetarian spring rolls made of?
How many calories in vegetarian crispy spring rolls?
What is the GI of spring rolls?
What is the difference between Cha Gio and Cha Gio Chay?

confidence_reason: No exact match was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN for this specific Vietnamese-style vegetarian crispy spring roll. Nutrient values were adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for fried vegetable spring rolls, which is compositionally analogous. GI was estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using analogous fried spring roll entries. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fried vegetable spring roll data; no exact HPB Singapore Foodo entry was identified for this dish. 2. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for fried spring rolls with vegetable filling; no direct measurement for Cha Gio Chay was found. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero for plant-based deep-fried products using vegetable oil per USDA data. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a fully plant-based dish containing no animal products. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a fully plant-based dish; B12 is not present in plant foods. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as no vitamin D-containing ingredients (animal products, fortified foods) are present. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy adapted values and are plausible for this food type. 8. The dish is listed under Singapore cuisine as it is served at a Singapore restaurant (Co Chung Restaurant), though the dish originates from Vietnamese culinary tradition. 9. Multilingual names provided for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and Vietnamese per Singaporean cuisine requirements.
